[[da:d:Q1053290]] Wikidata name: Kii Peninsula [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:11, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Largest peninsula on the island of Honshū in Japan.}} {{Infobox landform | water = | name = Kii Peninsula | other_name = | type = {{ill|Peninsula|en|Peninsula|WD=Q34763|lt=Peninsula}} | photo = Daimonzaka24 2048.jpg | photo_width = | photo_alt = | photo_caption = | map = | relief = | map_image = | map_caption = Location of the Kii Peninsula in Japan | location = [[:en:Kansai Region|Kansai Region]]: {{Plainlist| * {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}} * {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}} * {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture}} * {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture}}}} | coordinates = {{Coord|34|18|10|N|135|57|18|E|region:JP_type:isle|display=it}} | coordinates_ref = | range = | part_of = | water_bodies = | elevation_ft = | elevation_ref = | surface_elevation_ft = | surface_elevation_ref = | highest_point = | highest_elevation = | highest_coords = | length = | width = | area = | depth = | drop = | formed_by = | geology = | age = | orogeny = | volcanic_arc/belt = | volcanic_arc = | volcanic_belt = | volcanic_field = | eruption = | last_eruption = | topo = | operator = | designation = | free_label_1 = Rivers | free_data_1 = {{ill|Kinokawa River|en|Kinokawa River|WD=Q1042624|lt=Kinokawa River}}, {{ill|Kushida River|en|Kushida River|WD=Q6447936|lt=Kushida River}} | free_label_2 = | free_data_2 = | website = | embed = }} The {{Nihongo|'''Kii Peninsula'''|紀伊半島|Kii Hantō|lead=yes}} is the largest {{ill|Peninsula|en|Peninsula|WD=Q34763|lt=Peninsula}} on the island of [[Honshu|Honshū]] in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} and is located within the Kansai region.{{Cite web |url=http://www.coremoc.go.jp/english/pub/coralreefjapan/060203_kii.pdf |title=Kii Peninsula |access-date=2011-11-25 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120426001912/http://www.coremoc.go.jp/english/pub/coralreefjapan/060203_kii.pdf |archive-date=2012-04-26 |url-status=dead }} It is named after the ancient {{ill|Kii Province|en|Kii Province|WD=Q864053|lt=Kii Province}}. The peninsula has long been a sacred place in Buddhism, Shinto, and Shugendo, and many people would visit from all over Japan as part of the Kumano religious practices. == Overview == {{Location map|Japan|width=220|float=left|caption=Location of Kii Peninsula|label=Kii
Peninsula|position=bottom|mark=Red circle.svg|marksize=30|lat_deg=34|lat_min=08|lat_sec=10|lat_dir=N|lon_deg=135|lon_min=53|lon_sec=18|lon_dir=E}} The area south of the “{{ill|Japan Median Tectonic Line|en|Japan Median Tectonic Line|WD=Q1185053|lt=Central Tectonic Line}}” is called {{Nihongo|'''Nanki'''|南紀}}, and is home to reef-like coral communities which are amongst the northernmost in the world{{Cite book|title=Coral Reefs of Japan|publisher=Ministry of the Environment, Japan|year=2004}} (apart from [[:en:cold-water corals|cold-water corals]]{{Cite web|url=https://www.earthrangers.com/risk/coral-reefs-of-canada-pacific-coast-rocky-reefs/|title=Coral Reefs of Canada: Pacific Coast Rocky Reefs {{!}} Earth Rangers: Where kids go to save animals!|last=Rangers|first=Earth|date=2014-04-11|website=Earth Rangers|language=en-US|access-date=2020-01-17}}) due to the presence of the warm {{ill|Kuroshio Current|en|Kuroshio Current|WD=Q53842|lt=Kuroshio Current}}, though these are threatened by global warming{{Cite journal|last1=Pandolfi|first1=John M.|last2=Connolly|first2=Sean R.|last3=Marshall|first3=Dustin J.|last4=Cohen|first4=Anne L.|date=2011-07-22|title=Projecting Coral Reef Futures Under Global Warming and Ocean Acidification|url=https://www.science.org/doi/10.1126/science.1204794|journal=Science|language=en|volume=333|issue=6041|pages=418–422|doi=10.1126/science.1204794|issn=0036-8075|pmid=21778392|bibcode=2011Sci...333..418P|s2cid=29615751}} and human interference. Because of the Kuroshio’s strong influence, the climate of Nankii is the wettest in the Earth’s {{ill|subtropics|en|subtropics|WD=Q16305538|lt=subtropics}} with rainfall in the southern mountains believed to reach {{convert|5|m|in|-1}} per year and averaging {{convert|3.85|m|in|1}} in the southeastern town of [[:en:Owase, Mie|Owase]], comparable to {{ill|Ketchikan, Alaska|en|Ketchikan, Alaska|WD=Q43981|lt=Ketchikan}}, {{ill|Alaska|en|Alaska|WD=Q797|lt=Alaska}} or {{ill|Tortel|en|Tortel|WD=Q2390232|lt=Tortel}} in southern Chile. When {{ill|Typhoon|en|Typhoon|WD=Q140588|lt=Typhoon}}s hit Japan, the Kii Peninsula is typically the worst affected area and daily rainfalls as high as {{convert|940|mm|in|0}} are known so the Kii Peninsula is often referred to as the Typhoon Ginza (after {{ill|Ginza|en|Ginza|WD=Q746052|lt=Ginza}} in {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo}}). The peninsula was severely affected by the {{ill|Tropical Storm Talas (2011)|en|Tropical Storm Talas (2011)|WD=Q1360941|lt=2011 typhoon season}}, which caused severe damage and many deaths. A significant amount of damage remains today from the 2011 landslides caused by the typhoons.{{Cite web |title=Typhoon in the Kii Peninsula, Japan, September 2011 {{!}} GSI HOME PAGE |url=https://www.gsi.go.jp/kokusaikoryu/kokusaikoryu-e30008.html |access-date=2023-04-26 |website=www.gsi.go.jp}} The natural landscape of the Kii Peninsula is dense {{ill|temperate rainforest|en|temperate rainforest|WD=Q845725|lt=temperate rainforest}}, but the vast majority of forests are monoculture pine plantations which were planted to rebuild after the destruction of World War II. The region is well known for its citrus varieties and orchards. Much of the coast consists of networks of small {{ill|ria|en|ria|WD=Q554394|lt=ria}}s into which flow very steep and rapid streams characterised by numerous high {{ill|Waterfall|en|Waterfall|WD=Q34038|lt=Waterfall}}s. {{ill|Forestry|en|Forestry|WD=Q38112|lt=Forestry}} and {{ill|Fishing|en|Fishing|WD=Q14373|lt=Fishing}} were the traditional economic mainstays of the region and remain important even today despite a declining population and labour force. The region is affected by the dual-crises of severe population decline and widespread poverty.Kozue Taguchi; “Local Forestry and Sawmill Industries; The Case of Kumano, Mie Prefecture”, in Yoshiya Iwai; ''Forestry and the Forest Industry in Japan''; pp. 230–237. {{ISBN|0-7748-0883-7}} ==Location== {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}} occupies much of the area, including the entire southern part. To the northwest of Wakayama Prefecture is {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture}}, whose southern part is on the peninsula. East of Osaka Prefecture is landlocked {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture}}; farther east is {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}}. The [[Seto Inland Sea]] lies to the west of the Kii Peninsula. To the south and east is the Pacific Ocean and to the north is the valley of the {{ill|Kiso Three Rivers|en|Kiso Three Rivers|WD=Q852930|lt=Kiso Three Rivers}} and {{ill|Ise Bay|en|Ise Bay|WD=Q1368308|lt=Ise Bay}}. ==Notable places== Notable places in the Kii Peninsula include: * {{ill|Ise, Mie|en|Ise, Mie|WD=Q328067|lt=Ise}}, the location of the [[:en:Grand Shrine of Ise|Grand Shrine of Ise]] and center of pearl production. * [[Kumano Region]], home of the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Shrine]]s, the World Heritage [[Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodo]] pilgrimage routes and [[Nachi Falls]]. Another name is [[:en:Muro District|Muro District]]. * {{ill|Kushimoto, Wakayama|en|Kushimoto, Wakayama|WD=Q1343733|lt=Kushimoto, Wakayama}}, the southernmost point in Honshū. * {{ill|Mount Kōya|en|Mount Kōya|WD=Q535065|lt=Mount Kōya}} (or Kōyasan), the headquarters of the Shingon sect of Buddhism. * [[:en:Matsuzaka, Mie|Matsuzaka]], now the center of a major beef-producing area, formerly the center of [[Ise merchant]]s. * {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara}}, former [[:en:Capital (political)|capital]] of Japan. * {{ill|Shirahama, Wakayama|en|Shirahama, Wakayama|WD=Q1202728|lt=Shirahama}}, a resort town famous for its beachside onsens. * {{ill|Taiji, Wakayama|en|Taiji, Wakayama|WD=Q1023770|lt=Taiji, Wakayama}}, the birthplace of Japanese traditional whaling. * {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama}}, former home of the Kii (or Kishu) [[Tokugawa clan]]. It is the location of the [[Hinokuma Shrine]], which is affiliated to the Grand Shrine of Ise. * {{ill|Yoshino District, Nara|en|Yoshino District, Nara|WD=Q1154558|lt=Yoshino District}}, a wild area of heavily forested deep mountains, home of the Southern Imperial Court during the [[Nanboku-chō]] period of Japanese history. The Kii Peninsula is the location of a {{ill|UNESCO|en|UNESCO|WD=Q7809|lt=UNESCO}} {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|WD=Q9259|lt=World Heritage Site}}: {{ill|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|en|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|WD=Q611345|lt=Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range}}.[https://whc.unesco.org/en/list/1142 The List] In 2004, [[:en:United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization|UNESCO]] designated three other locations on the Kii Peninsula as World Heritage Sites. They are: #{{ill|Yoshino District, Nara|en|Yoshino District, Nara|WD=Q1154558|lt=Yoshino}} and [[:en:Mount Omine|Mount Omine]], mountainous areas in the north of the peninsula. #[[Kumano shrine|Kumano Shrine]]s, three shrines at the southern tip of the peninsula. #{{ill|Mount Kōya|en|Mount Kōya|WD=Q535065|lt=Mount Kōya}}, the mountain at the west of the peninsula. ==Transportation== * [[:en:Nanki-Shirahama Airport|Nanki-Shirahama Airport]] in {{ill|Shirahama, Wakayama|en|Shirahama, Wakayama|WD=Q1202728|lt=Shirahama}} serves the southern part of the Kii Peninsula. * The {{ill|Kisei Main Line|en|Kisei Main Line|WD=Q582803|lt=Kisei Main Line}} connects Wakayama to Mie Prefecture and runs along the peninsula's coastline. ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==External links== [[Category:11 commons]] [[Category:Category]] * [https://en.visitwakayama.jp/ Visit Wakayama] * [http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/index.html#2 Tanabe City Kumano Tourism Bureau] {{ill|Kii Province|en|Kii Province|WD=Q864053|lt=Kii Province}}. The peninsula has long been a sacred place in Buddhism, Shinto, and Shugendo, and many people would visit from all over Japan as part of the Kumano religious practices. == Overview == {{Location map|Japan|width=220|float=left|caption=Location of Kii Peninsula|label=Kii
Peninsula|position=bottom|mark=Red circle.svg|marksize=30|lat_deg=34|lat_min=08|lat_sec=10|lat_dir=N|lon_deg=135|lon_min=53|lon_sec=18|lon_dir=E}} The area south of the “{{ill|Japan Median Tectonic Line|en|Japan Median Tectonic Line|WD=Q1185053|lt=Central Tectonic Line}}” is called {{Nihongo|'''Nanki'''|南紀}}, and is home to reef-like coral communities which are amongst the northernmost in the world{{Cite book|title=Coral Reefs of Japan|publisher=Ministry of the Environment, Japan|year=2004}} (apart from [[:en:cold-water corals|cold-water corals]]{{Cite web|url=https://www.earthrangers.com/risk/coral-reefs-of-canada-pacific-coast-rocky-reefs/|title=Coral Reefs of Canada: Pacific Coast Rocky Reefs {{!}} Earth Rangers: Where kids go to save animals!|last=Rangers|first=Earth|date=2014-04-11|website=Earth Rangers|language=en-US|access-date=2020-01-17}}) due to the presence of the warm {{ill|Kuroshio Current|en|Kuroshio Current|WD=Q53842|lt=Kuroshio Current}}, though these are threatened by global warming{{Cite journal|last1=Pandolfi|first1=John M.|last2=Connolly|first2=Sean R.|last3=Marshall|first3=Dustin J.|last4=Cohen|first4=Anne L.|date=2011-07-22|title=Projecting Coral Reef Futures Under Global Warming and Ocean Acidification|url=https://www.science.org/doi/10.1126/science.1204794|journal=Science|language=en|volume=333|issue=6041|pages=418–422|doi=10.1126/science.1204794|issn=0036-8075|pmid=21778392|bibcode=2011Sci...333..418P|s2cid=29615751}} and human interference. Because of the Kuroshio’s strong influence, the climate of Nankii is the wettest in the Earth’s {{ill|subtropics|en|subtropics|WD=Q16305538|lt=subtropics}} with rainfall in the southern mountains believed to reach {{convert|5|m|in|-1}} per year and averaging {{convert|3.85|m|in|1}} in the southeastern town of [[:en:Owase, Mie|Owase]], comparable to {{ill|Ketchikan, Alaska|en|Ketchikan, Alaska|WD=Q43981|lt=Ketchikan}}, {{ill|Alaska|en|Alaska|WD=Q797|lt=Alaska}} or {{ill|Tortel|en|Tortel|WD=Q2390232|lt=Tortel}} in southern Chile. When {{ill|Typhoon|en|Typhoon|WD=Q140588|lt=Typhoon}}s hit Japan, the Kii Peninsula is typically the worst affected area and daily rainfalls as high as {{convert|940|mm|in|0}} are known so the Kii Peninsula is often referred to as the Typhoon Ginza (after {{ill|Ginza|en|Ginza|WD=Q746052|lt=Ginza}} in {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo}}). The peninsula was severely affected by the {{ill|Tropical Storm Talas (2011)|en|Tropical Storm Talas (2011)|WD=Q1360941|lt=2011 typhoon season}}, which caused severe damage and many deaths. A significant amount of damage remains today from the 2011 landslides caused by the typhoons.{{Cite web |title=Typhoon in the Kii Peninsula, Japan, September 2011 {{!}} GSI HOME PAGE |url=https://www.gsi.go.jp/kokusaikoryu/kokusaikoryu-e30008.html |access-date=2023-04-26 |website=www.gsi.go.jp}} The natural landscape of the Kii Peninsula is dense {{ill|temperate rainforest|en|temperate rainforest|WD=Q845725|lt=temperate rainforest}}, but the vast majority of forests are monoculture pine plantations which were planted to rebuild after the destruction of World War II. The region is well known for its citrus varieties and orchards. Much of the coast consists of networks of small {{ill|ria|en|ria|WD=Q554394|lt=ria}}s into which flow very steep and rapid streams characterised by numerous high {{ill|Waterfall|en|Waterfall|WD=Q34038|lt=Waterfall}}s. {{ill|Forestry|en|Forestry|WD=Q38112|lt=Forestry}} and {{ill|Fishing|en|Fishing|WD=Q14373|lt=Fishing}} were the traditional economic mainstays of the region and remain important even today despite a declining population and labour force. The region is affected by the dual-crises of severe population decline and widespread poverty.Kozue Taguchi; “Local Forestry and Sawmill Industries; The Case of Kumano, Mie Prefecture”, in Yoshiya Iwai; ''Forestry and the Forest Industry in Japan''; pp. 230–237. {{ISBN|0-7748-0883-7}} ==Location== {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}} occupies much of the area, including the entire southern part. To the northwest of Wakayama Prefecture is {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture}}, whose southern part is on the peninsula. East of Osaka Prefecture is landlocked {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture}}; farther east is {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}}. The [[Seto Inland Sea]] lies to the west of the Kii Peninsula. To the south and east is the Pacific Ocean and to the north is the valley of the {{ill|Kiso Three Rivers|en|Kiso Three Rivers|WD=Q852930|lt=Kiso Three Rivers}} and {{ill|Ise Bay|en|Ise Bay|WD=Q1368308|lt=Ise Bay}}. ==Notable places== Notable places in the Kii Peninsula include: * {{ill|Ise, Mie|en|Ise, Mie|WD=Q328067|lt=Ise}}, the location of the [[:en:Grand Shrine of Ise|Grand Shrine of Ise]] and center of pearl production. * [[Kumano Region]], home of the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Shrine]]s, the World Heritage [[Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodo]] pilgrimage routes and [[Nachi Falls]]. Another name is [[:en:Muro District|Muro District]]. * {{ill|Kushimoto, Wakayama|en|Kushimoto, Wakayama|WD=Q1343733|lt=Kushimoto, Wakayama}}, the southernmost point in Honshū. * {{ill|Mount Kōya|en|Mount Kōya|WD=Q535065|lt=Mount Kōya}} (or Kōyasan), the headquarters of the Shingon sect of Buddhism. * [[:en:Matsuzaka, Mie|Matsuzaka]], now the center of a major beef-producing area, formerly the center of [[Ise merchant]]s. * {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara}}, former [[:en:Capital (political)|capital]] of Japan. * {{ill|Shirahama, Wakayama|en|Shirahama, Wakayama|WD=Q1202728|lt=Shirahama}}, a resort town famous for its beachside onsens. * {{ill|Taiji, Wakayama|en|Taiji, Wakayama|WD=Q1023770|lt=Taiji, Wakayama}}, the birthplace of Japanese traditional whaling. * {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama}}, former home of the Kii (or Kishu) [[Tokugawa clan]]. It is the location of the [[Hinokuma Shrine]], which is affiliated to the Grand Shrine of Ise. * {{ill|Yoshino District, Nara|en|Yoshino District, Nara|WD=Q1154558|lt=Yoshino District}}, a wild area of heavily forested deep mountains, home of the Southern Imperial Court during the [[Nanboku-chō]] period of Japanese history. The Kii Peninsula is the location of a {{ill|UNESCO|en|UNESCO|WD=Q7809|lt=UNESCO}} {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|WD=Q9259|lt=World Heritage Site}}: {{ill|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|en|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|WD=Q611345|lt=Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range}}.[https://whc.unesco.org/en/list/1142 The List] In 2004, [[:en:United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization|UNESCO]] designated three other locations on the Kii Peninsula as World Heritage Sites. They are: #{{ill|Yoshino District, Nara|en|Yoshino District, Nara|WD=Q1154558|lt=Yoshino}} and [[:en:Mount Omine|Mount Omine]], mountainous areas in the north of the peninsula. #[[Kumano shrine|Kumano Shrine]]s, three shrines at the southern tip of the peninsula. #{{ill|Mount Kōya|en|Mount Kōya|WD=Q535065|lt=Mount Kōya}}, the mountain at the west of the peninsula. ==Transportation== * [[:en:Nanki-Shirahama Airport|Nanki-Shirahama Airport]] in {{ill|Shirahama, Wakayama|en|Shirahama, Wakayama|WD=Q1202728|lt=Shirahama}} serves the southern part of the Kii Peninsula. * The {{ill|Kisei Main Line|en|Kisei Main Line|WD=Q582803|lt=Kisei Main Line}} connects Wakayama to Mie Prefecture and runs along the peninsula's coastline. ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==External links== [[Category:11 commons]] [[Category:Category]] * [https://en.visitwakayama.jp/ Visit Wakayama] * [http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/index.html#2 Tanabe City Kumano Tourism Bureau] (полуостров)]] [[ceb:Kii Hantō]] [[cs:Kii (poloostrov)]] [[de:Kii-Halbinsel]] [[es:Península de Kii]] [[eu:Kii penintsula]] [[fa:شبه‌جزیره کی]] [[fi:Kiin niemimaa]] [[fr:Péninsule de Kii]] [[he:חצי Peninsula'''|紀伊半島|Kii Hantō|lead=yes}} is the largest {{ill|Peninsula|en|Peninsula|WD=Q34763|lt=Peninsula}} on the island of [[Honshu|Honshū]] in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} and is located within the Kansai region.{{Cite web |url=http://www.coremoc.go.jp/english/pub/coralreefjapan/060203_kii.pdf |title=Kii Peninsula |access-date=2011-11-25 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120426001912/http://www.coremoc.go.jp/english/pub/coralreefjapan/060203_kii.pdf |archive-date=2012-04-26 |url-status=dead }} It is named after the ancient {{ill|Kii Province|en|Kii Province|WD=Q864053|lt=Kii Province}}. The peninsula has long been a sacred place in Buddhism, Shinto, and Shugendo, and many people would visit from all over Japan as part of the Kumano religious practices. == Overview == {{Location map|Japan|width=220|float=left|caption=Location of Kii Peninsula|label=Kii
Peninsula|position=bottom|mark=Red circle.svg|marksize=30|lat_deg=34|lat_min=08|lat_sec=10|lat_dir=N|lon_deg=135|lon_min=53|lon_sec=18|lon_dir=E}} The area south of the “{{ill|Japan Median Tectonic Line|en|Japan Median Tectonic Line|WD=Q1185053|lt=Central Tectonic Line}}” is called {{Nihongo|'''Nanki'''|南紀}}, and is home to reef-like coral communities which are amongst the northernmost in the world{{Cite book|title=Coral Reefs of Japan|publisher=Ministry of the Environment, Japan|year=2004}} (apart from [[:en:cold-water corals|cold-water corals]]{{Cite web|url=https://www.earthrangers.com/risk/coral-reefs-of-canada-pacific-coast-rocky-reefs/|title=Coral Reefs of Canada: Pacific Coast Rocky Reefs {{!}} Earth Rangers: Where kids go to save animals!|last=Rangers|first=Earth|date=2014-04-11|website=Earth Rangers|language=en-US|access-date=2020-01-17}}) due to the presence of the warm {{ill|Kuroshio Current|en|Kuroshio Current|WD=Q53842|lt=Kuroshio Current}}, though these are threatened by global warming{{Cite journal|last1=Pandolfi|first1=John M.|last2=Connolly|first2=Sean R.|last3=Marshall|first3=Dustin J.|last4=Cohen|first4=Anne L.|date=2011-07-22|title=Projecting Coral Reef Futures Under Global Warming and Ocean Acidification|url=https://www.science.org/doi/10.1126/science.1204794|journal=Science|language=en|volume=333|issue=6041|pages=418–422|doi=10.1126/science.1204794|issn=0036-8075|pmid=21778392|bibcode=2011Sci...333..418P|s2cid=29615751}} and human interference. Because of the Kuroshio’s strong influence, the climate of Nankii is the wettest in the Earth’s {{ill|subtropics|en|subtropics|WD=Q16305538|lt=subtropics}} with rainfall in the southern mountains believed to reach {{convert|5|m|in|-1}} per year and averaging {{convert|3.85|m|in|1}} in the southeastern town of [[:en:Owase, Mie|Owase]], comparable to {{ill|Ketchikan, Alaska|en|Ketchikan, Alaska|WD=Q43981|lt=Ketchikan}}, {{ill|Alaska|en|Alaska|WD=Q797|lt=Alaska}} or {{ill|Tortel|en|Tortel|WD=Q2390232|lt=Tortel}} in southern Chile. When {{ill|Typhoon|en|Typhoon|WD=Q140588|lt=Typhoon}}s hit Japan, the Kii Peninsula is typically the worst affected area and daily rainfalls as high as {{convert|940|mm|in|0}} are known so the Kii Peninsula is often referred to as the Typhoon Ginza (after {{ill|Ginza|en|Ginza|WD=Q746052|lt=Ginza}} in {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo}}). The peninsula was severely affected by the {{ill|Tropical Storm Talas (2011)|en|Tropical Storm Talas (2011)|WD=Q1360941|lt=2011 typhoon season}}, which caused severe damage and many deaths. A significant amount of damage remains today from the 2011 landslides caused by the typhoons.{{Cite web |title=Typhoon in the Kii Peninsula, Japan, September 2011 {{!}} GSI HOME PAGE |url=https://www.gsi.go.jp/kokusaikoryu/kokusaikoryu-e30008.html |access-date=2023-04-26 |website=www.gsi.go.jp}} The natural landscape of the Kii Peninsula is dense {{ill|temperate rainforest|en|temperate rainforest|WD=Q845725|lt=temperate rainforest}}, but the vast majority of forests are monoculture pine plantations which were planted to rebuild after the destruction of World War II. The region is well known for its citrus varieties and orchards. Much of the coast consists of networks of small {{ill|ria|en|ria|WD=Q554394|lt=ria}}s into which flow very steep and rapid streams characterised by numerous high {{ill|Waterfall|en|Waterfall|WD=Q34038|lt=Waterfall}}s. {{ill|Forestry|en|Forestry|WD=Q38112|lt=Forestry}} and {{ill|Fishing|en|Fishing|WD=Q14373|lt=Fishing}} were the traditional economic mainstays of the region and remain important even today despite a declining population and labour force. The region is affected by the dual-crises of severe population decline and widespread poverty.Kozue Taguchi; “Local Forestry and Sawmill Industries; The Case of Kumano, Mie Prefecture”, in Yoshiya Iwai; ''Forestry and the Forest Industry in Japan''; pp. 230–237. {{ISBN|0-7748-0883-7}} ==Location== {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}} occupies much of the area, including the entire southern part. To the northwest of Wakayama Prefecture is {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture}}, whose southern part is on the peninsula. East of Osaka Prefecture is landlocked {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture}}; farther east is {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}}. The [[Seto Inland Sea]] lies to the west of the Kii Peninsula. To the south and east is the Pacific Ocean and to the north is the valley of the {{ill|Kiso Three Rivers|en|Kiso Three Rivers|WD=Q852930|lt=Kiso Three Rivers}} and {{ill|Ise Bay|en|Ise Bay|WD=Q1368308|lt=Ise Bay}}. ==Notable places== Notable places in the Kii Peninsula include: * {{ill|Ise, Mie|en|Ise, Mie|WD=Q328067|lt=Ise}}, the location of the [[:en:Grand Shrine of Ise|Grand Shrine of Ise]] and center of pearl production. * [[Kumano Region]], home of the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Shrine]]s, the World Heritage [[Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodo]] pilgrimage routes and [[Nachi Falls]]. Another name is [[:en:Muro District|Muro District]]. * {{ill|Kushimoto, Wakayama|en|Kushimoto, Wakayama|WD=Q1343733|lt=Kushimoto, Wakayama}}, the southernmost point in Honshū. * {{ill|Mount Kōya|en|Mount Kōya|WD=Q535065|lt=Mount Kōya}} (or Kōyasan), the headquarters of the Shingon sect of Buddhism. * [[:en:Matsuzaka, Mie|Matsuzaka]], now the center of a major beef-producing area, formerly the center of [[Ise merchant]]s. * {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara}}, former [[:en:Capital (political)|capital]] of Japan. * {{ill|Shirahama, Wakayama|en|Shirahama, Wakayama|WD=Q1202728|lt=Shirahama}}, a resort town famous for its beachside onsens. * {{ill|Taiji, Wakayama|en|Taiji, Wakayama|WD=Q1023770|lt=Taiji, Wakayama}}, the birthplace of Japanese traditional whaling. * {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama}}, former home of the Kii (or Kishu) [[Tokugawa clan]]. It is the location of the [[Hinokuma Shrine]], which is affiliated to the Grand Shrine of Ise. * {{ill|Yoshino District, Nara|en|Yoshino District, Nara|WD=Q1154558|lt=Yoshino District}}, a wild area of heavily forested deep mountains, home of the Southern Imperial Court during the [[Nanboku-chō]] period of Japanese history. They are: #{{ill|Yoshino District, Nara|en|Yoshino District, Nara|WD=Q1154558|lt=Yoshino}} and [[:en:Mount Omine|Mount Omine]], mountainous areas in the north of the peninsula. #[[Kumano shrine|Kumano Shrine]]s, three shrines at the southern tip of the peninsula. #{{ill|Mount Kōya|en|Mount Kōya|WD=Q535065|lt=Mount Kōya}}, the mountain at the west of the peninsula. ==Transportation== * [[:en:Nanki-Shirahama Airport|Nanki-Shirahama Airport]] in {{ill|Shirahama, Wakayama|en|Shirahama, Wakayama|WD=Q1202728|lt=Shirahama}} serves the southern part of the Kii Peninsula. * The {{ill|Kisei Main Line|en|Kisei Main Line|WD=Q582803|lt=Kisei Main Line}} connects Wakayama to Mie Prefecture and runs along the peninsula's coastline. ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==External links== [[Category:11 commons]] [[Category:Category]] * [https://en.visitwakayama.jp/ Visit Wakayama] * [http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/index.html#2 Tanabe City Kumano Tourism Bureau] [[ar:شبه جزيرة كي]][[az:Kii yarımadası]][[bg:Кии (полуостров)]][[ceb:Kii Hantō]][[cs:Kii (poloostrov)]][[de:Kii-Halbinsel]][[en:Kii Peninsula]][[es:Península de Kii]][[eu:Kii (schiereiland)]][[pl:Kii (półwysep)]][[pt:Península de Kii]][[ru:Кии (полуостров)]][[simple:Kii Peninsula]][[sv:Kiihalvön]][[th:คาบสมุทรคิอิ]][[tr:Kii Yarımadası]][[vi:Bán đảo Kii]][[zh:紀伊半島]] {{short description|Largest peninsula on the island of Honshū in Japan.}} {{Infobox landform | water = | name = Kii Peninsula | other_name = | type = {{ill|Peninsula|en|Peninsula|WD=Q34763|lt=Peninsula}} | photo = Daimonzaka24 2048.jpg | photo_width = | photo_alt = | photo_caption = | map = | relief = | map_image = | map_caption = Location of the Kii Peninsula in Japan | location = [[:en:Kansai Region|Kansai Region]]: {{Plainlist| * {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}} * {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}} * {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture}} * {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture}}}} | coordinates = {{Coord|34|18|10|N|135|57|18|E|region:JP_type:isle|display=it}} | coordinates_ref = | range = | part_of = | water_bodies = | elevation_ft = | elevation_ref = | surface_elevation_ft = | surface_elevation_ref = | highest_point = | highest_elevation = | highest_coords = | length = | width = | area = | depth = | drop = | formed_by = | geology = | age = | orogeny = | volcanic_arc/belt = | volcanic_arc = | volcanic_belt = | volcanic_field = | eruption = | last_eruption = | topo = | operator = | designation = | free_label_1 = Rivers | free_data_1 = {{ill|Kinokawa River|en|Kinokawa River|WD=Q1042624|lt=Kinokawa River}}, {{ill|Kushida River|en|Kushida River|WD=Q6447936|lt=Kushida River}} | free_label_2 = | free_data_2 = | website = | embed = }} The {{Nihongo|'''Kii Peninsula'''|紀伊半島|Kii Hantō|lead=yes}} is the largest {{ill|Peninsula|en|Peninsula|WD=Q34763|lt=Peninsula}} on the island of [[Honshu|Honshū]] in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} and is located within the Kansai region.{{Cite web |url=http://www.coremoc.go.jp/english/pub/coralreefjapan/060203_kii.pdf |title=Kii Peninsula |access-date=2011-11-25 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120426001912/http://www.coremoc.go.jp/english/pub/coralreefjapan/060203_kii.pdf |archive-date=2012-04-26 |url-status=dead }} It is named after the ancient {{ill|Kii Province|en|Kii Province|WD=Q864053|lt=Kii Province}}. The peninsula has long been a sacred place in Buddhism, Shinto, and Shugendo, and many people would visit from all over Japan as part of the Kumano religious practices. == Overview == {{Location map|Japan|width=220|float=left|caption=Location of Kii Peninsula|label=Kii
Peninsula|position=bottom|mark=Red circle.svg|marksize=30|lat_deg=34|lat_min=08|lat_sec=10|lat_dir=N|lon_deg=135|lon_min=53|lon_sec=18|lon_dir=E}} The area south of the “{{ill|Japan Median Tectonic Line|en|Japan Median Tectonic Line|WD=Q1185053|lt=Central Tectonic Line}}” is called {{Nihongo|'''Nanki'''|南紀}}, and is home to reef-like coral communities which are amongst the northernmost in the world{{Cite book|title=Coral Reefs of Japan|publisher=Ministry of the Environment, Japan|year=2004}} (apart from [[:en:cold-water corals|cold-water corals]]{{Cite web|url=https://www.earthrangers.com/risk/coral-reefs-of-canada-pacific-coast-rocky-reefs/|title=Coral Reefs of Canada: Pacific Coast Rocky Reefs {{!}} Earth Rangers: Where kids go to save animals!|last=Rangers|first=Earth|date=2014-04-11|website=Earth Rangers|language=en-US|access-date=2020-01-17}}) due to the presence of the warm {{ill|Kuroshio Current|en|Kuroshio Current|WD=Q53842|lt=Kuroshio Current}}, though these are threatened by global warming{{Cite journal|last1=Pandolfi|first1=John M.|last2=Connolly|first2=Sean R.|last3=Marshall|first3=Dustin J.|last4=Cohen|first4=Anne L.|date=2011-07-22|title=Projecting Coral Reef Futures Under Global Warming and Ocean Acidification|url=https://www.science.org/doi/10.1126/science.1204794|journal=Science|language=en|volume=333|issue=6041|pages=418–422|doi=10.1126/science.1204794|issn=0036-8075|pmid=21778392|bibcode=2011Sci...333..418P|s2cid=29615751}} and human interference. Because of the Kuroshio’s strong influence, the climate of Nankii is the wettest in the Earth’s {{ill|subtropics|en|subtropics|WD=Q16305538|lt=subtropics}} with rainfall in the southern mountains believed to reach {{convert|5|m|in|-1}} per year and averaging {{convert|3.85|m|in|1}} in the southeastern town of [[:en:Owase, Mie|Owase]], comparable to {{ill|Ketchikan, Alaska|en|Ketchikan, Alaska|WD=Q43981|lt=Ketchikan}}, {{ill|Alaska|en|Alaska|WD=Q797|lt=Alaska}} or {{ill|Tortel|en|Tortel|WD=Q2390232|lt=Tortel}} in southern Chile. When {{ill|Typhoon|en|Typhoon|WD=Q140588|lt=Typhoon}}s hit Japan, the Kii Peninsula is typically the worst affected area and daily rainfalls as high as {{convert|940|mm|in|0}} are known so the Kii Peninsula is often referred to as the Typhoon Ginza (after {{ill|Ginza|en|Ginza|WD=Q746052|lt=Ginza}} in {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo}}). The peninsula was severely affected by the {{ill|Tropical Storm Talas (2011)|en|Tropical Storm Talas (2011)|WD=Q1360941|lt=2011 typhoon season}}, which caused severe damage and many deaths. A significant amount of damage remains today from the 2011 landslides caused by the typhoons.{{Cite web |title=Typhoon in the Kii Peninsula, Japan, September 2011 {{!}} GSI HOME PAGE |url=https://www.gsi.go.jp/kokusaikoryu/kokusaikoryu-e30008.html |access-date=2023-04-26 |website=www.gsi.go.jp}} The natural landscape of the Kii Peninsula is dense {{ill|temperate rainforest|en|temperate rainforest|WD=Q845725|lt=temperate rainforest}}, but the vast majority of forests are monoculture pine plantations which were planted to rebuild after the destruction of World War II. The region is well known for its citrus varieties and orchards. Much of the coast consists of networks of small {{ill|ria|en|ria|WD=Q554394|lt=ria}}s into which flow very steep and rapid streams characterised by numerous high {{ill|Waterfall|en|Waterfall|WD=Q34038|lt=Waterfall}}s. {{ill|Forestry|en|Forestry|WD=Q38112|lt=Forestry}} and {{ill|Fishing|en|Fishing|WD=Q14373|lt=Fishing}} were the traditional economic mainstays of the region and remain important even today despite a declining population and labour force. The region is affected by the dual-crises of severe population decline and widespread poverty.Kozue Taguchi; “Local Forestry and Sawmill Industries; The Case of Kumano, Mie Prefecture”, in Yoshiya Iwai; ''Forestry and the Forest Industry in Japan''; pp. 230–237. {{ISBN|0-7748-0883-7}} ==Location== {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}} occupies much of the area, including the entire southern part. To the northwest of Wakayama Prefecture is {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture}}, whose southern part is on the peninsula. East of Osaka Prefecture is landlocked {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture}}; farther east is {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}}. The [[Seto Inland Sea]] lies to the west of the Kii Peninsula. To the south and east is the Pacific Ocean and to the north is the valley of the {{ill|Kiso Three Rivers|en|Kiso Three Rivers|WD=Q852930|lt=Kiso Three Rivers}} and {{ill|Ise Bay|en|Ise Bay|WD=Q1368308|lt=Ise Bay}}. ==Notable places== Notable places in the Kii Peninsula include: * {{ill|Ise, Mie|en|Ise, Mie|WD=Q328067|lt=Ise}}, the location of the [[:en:Grand Shrine of Ise|Grand Shrine of Ise]] and center of pearl production. * [[Kumano Region]], home of the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Shrine]]s, the World Heritage [[Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodo]] pilgrimage routes and [[Nachi Falls]]. Another name is [[:en:Muro District|Muro District]]. * {{ill|Kushimoto, Wakayama|en|Kushimoto, Wakayama|WD=Q1343733|lt=Kushimoto, Wakayama}}, the southernmost point in Honshū. * {{ill|Mount Kōya|en|Mount Kōya|WD=Q535065|lt=Mount Kōya}} (or Kōyasan), the headquarters of the Shingon sect of Buddhism. * [[:en:Matsuzaka, Mie|Matsuzaka]], now the center of a major beef-producing area, formerly the center of [[Ise merchant]]s. * {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara}}, former [[:en:Capital (political)|capital]] of Japan. * {{ill|Shirahama, Wakayama|en|Shirahama, Wakayama|WD=Q1202728|lt=Shirahama}}, a resort town famous for its beachside onsens. * {{ill|Taiji, Wakayama|en|Taiji, Wakayama|WD=Q1023770|lt=Taiji, Wakayama}}, the birthplace of Japanese traditional whaling. * {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama}}, former home of the Kii (or Kishu) [[Tokugawa clan]]. It is the location of the [[Hinokuma Shrine]], which is affiliated to the Grand Shrine of Ise. * {{ill|Yoshino District, Nara|en|Yoshino District, Nara|WD=Q1154558|lt=Yoshino District}}, a wild area of heavily forested deep mountains, home of the Southern Imperial Court during the [[Nanboku-chō]] period of Japanese history. The Kii Peninsula is the location of a {{ill|UNESCO|en|UNESCO|WD=Q7809|lt=UNESCO}} {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|WD=Q9259|lt=World Heritage Site}}: {{ill|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|en|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|WD=Q611345|lt=Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range}}.[https://whc.unesco.org/en/list/1142 The List] In 2004, [[:en:United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization|UNESCO]] designated three other locations on the Kii Peninsula as World Heritage Sites. They are: #{{ill|Yoshino District, Nara|en|Yoshino District, Nara|WD=Q1154558|lt=Yoshino}} and [[:en:Mount Omine|Mount Omine]], mountainous areas in the north of the peninsula. #[[Kumano shrine|Kumano Shrine]]s, three shrines at the southern tip of the peninsula. #{{ill|Mount Kōya|en|Mount Kōya|WD=Q535065|lt=Mount Kōya}}, the mountain at the west of the peninsula. ==Transportation== * [[:en:Nanki-Shirahama Airport|Nanki-Shirahama Airport]] in {{ill|Shirahama, Wakayama|en|Shirahama, Wakayama|WD=Q1202728|lt=Shirahama}} serves the southern part of the Kii Peninsula. * The {{ill|Kisei Main Line|en|Kisei Main Line|WD=Q582803|lt=Kisei Main Line}} connects Wakayama to Mie Prefecture and runs along the peninsula's coastline. ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==External links== [[Category:Category]] * [https://en.visitwakayama.jp/ Visit Wakayama] * [http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/index.html#2 Tanabe City Kumano Tourism Bureau] The peninsula has long been a sacred place in Buddhism, Shinto, and Shugendo, and many people would visit from all over Japan as part of the Kumano religious practices. == Overview == {{Location map|Japan|width=220|float=left|caption=Location of Kii Peninsula|label=Kii
Peninsula|position=bottom|mark=Red circle.svg|marksize=30|lat_deg=34|lat_min=08|lat_sec=10|lat_dir=N|lon_deg=135|lon_min=53|lon_sec=18|lon_dir=E}} The area south of the “{{ill|Japan Median Tectonic Line|en|Japan Median Tectonic Line|WD=Q1185053|lt=Central Tectonic Line}}” is called {{Nihongo|'''Nanki'''|南紀}}, and is home to reef-like coral communities which are amongst the northernmost in the world{{Cite book|title=Coral Reefs of Japan|publisher=Ministry of the Environment, Japan|year=2004}} (apart from [[:en:cold-water corals|cold-water corals]]{{Cite web|url=https://www.earthrangers.com/risk/coral-reefs-of-canada-pacific-coast-rocky-reefs/|title=Coral Reefs of Canada: Pacific Coast Rocky Reefs {{!}} Earth Rangers: Where kids go to save animals!|last=Rangers|first=Earth|date=2014-04-11|website=Earth Rangers|language=en-US|access-date=2020-01-17}}) due to the presence of the warm {{ill|Kuroshio Current|en|Kuroshio Current|WD=Q53842|lt=Kuroshio Current}}, though these are threatened by global warming{{Cite journal|last1=Pandolfi|first1=John M.|last2=Connolly|first2=Sean R.|last3=Marshall|first3=Dustin J.|last4=Cohen|first4=Anne L.|date=2011-07-22|title=Projecting Coral Reef Futures Under Global Warming and Ocean Acidification|url=https://www.science.org/doi/10.1126/science.1204794|journal=Science|language=en|volume=333|issue=6041|pages=418–422|doi=10.1126/science.1204794|issn=0036-8075|pmid=21778392|bibcode=2011Sci...333..418P|s2cid=29615751}} and human interference. Because of the Kuroshio’s strong influence, the climate of Nankii is the wettest in the Earth’s {{ill|subtropics|en|subtropics|WD=Q16305538|lt=subtropics}} with rainfall in the southern mountains believed to reach {{convert|5|m|in|-1}} per year and averaging {{convert|3.85|m|in|1}} in the southeastern town of [[:en:Owase, Mie|Owase]], comparable to {{ill|Ketchikan, Alaska|en|Ketchikan, Alaska|WD=Q43981|lt=Ketchikan}}, {{ill|Alaska|en|Alaska|WD=Q797|lt=Alaska}} or {{ill|Tortel|en|Tortel|WD=Q2390232|lt=Tortel}} in southern Chile. When {{ill|Typhoon|en|Typhoon|WD=Q140588|lt=Typhoon}}s hit Japan, the Kii Peninsula is typically the worst affected area and daily rainfalls as high as {{convert|940|mm|in|0}} are known so the Kii Peninsula is often referred to as the Typhoon Ginza (after {{ill|Ginza|en|Ginza|WD=Q746052|lt=Ginza}} in {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo}}). The peninsula was severely affected by the {{ill|Tropical Storm Talas (2011)|en|Tropical Storm Talas (2011)|WD=Q1360941|lt=2011 typhoon season}}, which caused severe damage and many deaths. A significant amount of damage remains today from the 2011 landslides caused by the typhoons.{{Cite web |title=Typhoon in the Kii Peninsula, Japan, September 2011 {{!}} GSI HOME PAGE |url=https://www.gsi.go.jp/kokusaikoryu/kokusaikoryu-e30008.html |access-date=2023-04-26 |website=www.gsi.go.jp}} The natural landscape of the Kii Peninsula is dense {{ill|temperate rainforest|en|temperate rainforest|WD=Q845725|lt=temperate rainforest}}, but the vast majority of forests are monoculture pine plantations which were planted to rebuild after the destruction of World War II. The region is well known for its citrus varieties and orchards. Much of the coast consists of networks of small {{ill|ria|en|ria|WD=Q554394|lt=ria}}s into which flow very steep and rapid streams characterised by numerous high {{ill|Waterfall|en|Waterfall|WD=Q34038|lt=Waterfall}}s. {{ill|Forestry|en|Forestry|WD=Q38112|lt=Forestry}} and {{ill|Fishing|en|Fishing|WD=Q14373|lt=Fishing}} were the traditional economic mainstays of the region and remain important even today despite a declining population and labour force. The region is affected by the dual-crises of severe population decline and widespread poverty.Kozue Taguchi; “Local Forestry and Sawmill Industries; The Case of Kumano, Mie Prefecture”, in Yoshiya Iwai; ''Forestry and the Forest Industry in Japan''; pp. 230–237. {{ISBN|0-7748-0883-7}} ==Location== {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}} occupies much of the area, including the entire southern part. To the northwest of Wakayama Prefecture is {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture}}, whose southern part is on the peninsula. East of Osaka Prefecture is landlocked {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture}}; farther east is {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}}. The [[Seto Inland Sea]] lies to the west of the Kii Peninsula. To the south and east is the Pacific Ocean and to the north is the valley of the {{ill|Kiso Three Rivers|en|Kiso Three Rivers|WD=Q852930|lt=Kiso Three Rivers}} and {{ill|Ise Bay|en|Ise Bay|WD=Q1368308|lt=Ise Bay}}. ==Notable places== Notable places in the Kii Peninsula include: * {{ill|Ise, Mie|en|Ise, Mie|WD=Q328067|lt=Ise}}, the location of the [[:en:Grand Shrine of Ise|Grand Shrine of Ise]] and center of pearl production. * [[Kumano Region]], home of the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Shrine]]s, the World Heritage [[Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodo]] pilgrimage routes and [[Nachi Falls]]. Another name is [[:en:Muro District|Muro District]]. * {{ill|Kushimoto, Wakayama|en|Kushimoto, Wakayama|WD=Q1343733|lt=Kushimoto, Wakayama}}, the southernmost point in Honshū. * {{ill|Mount Kōya|en|Mount Kōya|WD=Q535065|lt=Mount Kōya}} (or Kōyasan), the headquarters of the Shingon sect of Buddhism. * [[:en:Matsuzaka, Mie|Matsuzaka]], now the center of a major beef-producing area, formerly the center of [[Ise merchant]]s. * {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara}}, former [[:en:Capital (political)|capital]] of Japan. * {{ill|Shirahama, Wakayama|en|Shirahama, Wakayama|WD=Q1202728|lt=Shirahama}}, a resort town famous for its beachside onsens. * {{ill|Taiji, Wakayama|en|Taiji, Wakayama|WD=Q1023770|lt=Taiji, Wakayama}}, the birthplace of Japanese traditional whaling. * {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama}}, former home of the Kii (or Kishu) [[Tokugawa clan]]. It is the location of the [[Hinokuma Shrine]], which is affiliated to the Grand Shrine of Ise. * {{ill|Yoshino District, Nara|en|Yoshino District, Nara|WD=Q1154558|lt=Yoshino District}}, a wild area of heavily forested deep mountains, home of the Southern Imperial Court during the [[Nanboku-chō]] period of Japanese history. The Kii Peninsula is the location of a {{ill|UNESCO|en|UNESCO|WD=Q7809|lt=UNESCO}} {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|WD=Q9259|lt=World Heritage Site}}: {{ill|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|en|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|WD=Q611345|lt=Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range}}.[https://whc.unesco.org/en/list/1142 The List] In 2004, [[:en:United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization|UNESCO]] designated three other locations on the Kii Peninsula as World Heritage Sites. They are: #{{ill|Yoshino District, Nara|en|Yoshino District, Nara|WD=Q1154558|lt=Yoshino}} and [[:en:Mount Omine|Mount Omine]], mountainous areas in the north of the peninsula. #[[Kumano shrine|Kumano Shrine]]s, three shrines at the southern tip of the peninsula. #{{ill|Mount Kōya|en|Mount Kōya|WD=Q535065|lt=Mount Kōya}}, the mountain at the west of the peninsula. ==Transportation== * [[:en:Nanki-Shirahama Airport|Nanki-Shirahama Airport]] in {{ill|Shirahama, Wakayama|en|Shirahama, Wakayama|WD=Q1202728|lt=Shirahama}} serves the southern part of the Kii Peninsula. * The {{ill|Kisei Main Line|en|Kisei Main Line|WD=Q582803|lt=Kisei Main Line}} connects Wakayama to Mie Prefecture and runs along the peninsula's coastline. ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==External links== * [https://en.visitwakayama.jp/ Visit Wakayama] * [http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/index.html#2 Tanabe City Kumano Tourism Bureau] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110925191301/http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/index.html#2 |date=2011-09-25 }} [[en:Kii Peninsula]] [[ar:شبه جزيرة كي]] [[az:Kii yarımadası]] [[bg:Кии (полуостров)]] [[ceb:Kii Hantō]] [[cs:Kii (poloostrov)]] [[de:Kii-Halbinsel]] [[es:Península de Kii]] [[eu:Kii penintsula]] [[fa:شبه‌جزیره کی]] [[fi:Kiin niemimaa]] [[fr:Péninsule de Kii]] [[he:חצי האי קיאי]] [[hr:Kii]] [[id:Semenanjung Kii]] [[it:Penisola di Kii]] [[ja:紀伊半島]] [[ko:기이반도]] [[lt:Kijo pusiasalis]] [[nl:Kii (schiereiland)]] [[pl:Kii (półwysep)]] [[pt:Península de Kii]] [[ru:Кии (полуостров)]] [[simple:Kii Peninsula]] [[sv:Kiihalvön]] [[th:คาบสมุทรคิอิ]] [[tr:Kii Yarımadası]] [[vi:Bán đảo Kii]] [[zh:紀伊半島]] {{wikidata link|Q1053290}} [[Category:Kii Peninsula]] [[Category:Landforms of Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Landforms of Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Landforms of Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]] [[Category:Peninsulas of Japan]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

Peninsula'''|紀伊半島|Kii Hantō|lead=yes}} is the largest {{ill|Peninsula|en|Peninsula|WD=Q34763|lt=Peninsula}} on the island of [[Honshu|Honshū]] in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} and is located within the Kansai region.{{Cite web |url=http://www.coremoc.go.jp/english/pub/coralreefjapan/060203_kii.pdf |title=Kii Peninsula |access-date=2011-11-25 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120426001912/http://www.coremoc.go.jp/english/pub/coralreefjapan/060203_kii.pdf |archive-date=2012-04-26 |url-status=dead }} It is named after the ancient {{ill|Kii Province|en|Kii Province|WD=Q864053|lt=Kii Province}}. The peninsula has long been a sacred place in Buddhism, Shinto, and Shugendo, and many people would visit from all over Japan as part of the Kumano religious practices. == Overview == {{Location map|Japan|width=220|float=left|caption=Location of Kii Peninsula|label=Kii
Peninsula|position=bottom|mark=Red circle.svg|marksize=30|lat_deg=34|lat_min=08|lat_sec=10|lat_dir=N|lon_deg=135|lon_min=53|lon_sec=18|lon_dir=E}} The area south of the “{{ill|Japan Median Tectonic Line|en|Japan Median Tectonic Line|WD=Q1185053|lt=Central Tectonic Line}}” is called {{Nihongo|'''Nanki'''|南紀}}, and is home to reef-like coral communities which are amongst the northernmost in the world{{Cite book|title=Coral Reefs of Japan|publisher=Ministry of the Environment, Japan|year=2004}} (apart from [[:en:cold-water corals|cold-water corals]]{{Cite web|url=https://www.earthrangers.com/risk/coral-reefs-of-canada-pacific-coast-rocky-reefs/|title=Coral Reefs of Canada: Pacific Coast Rocky Reefs {{!}} Earth Rangers: Where kids go to save animals!|last=Rangers|first=Earth|date=2014-04-11|website=Earth Rangers|language=en-US|access-date=2020-01-17}}) due to the presence of the warm {{ill|Kuroshio Current|en|Kuroshio Current|WD=Q53842|lt=Kuroshio Current}}, though these are threatened by global warming{{Cite journal|last1=Pandolfi|first1=John M.|last2=Connolly|first2=Sean R.|last3=Marshall|first3=Dustin J.|last4=Cohen|first4=Anne L.|date=2011-07-22|title=Projecting Coral Reef Futures Under Global Warming and Ocean Acidification|url=https://www.science.org/doi/10.1126/science.1204794|journal=Science|language=en|volume=333|issue=6041|pages=418–422|doi=10.1126/science.1204794|issn=0036-8075|pmid=21778392|bibcode=2011Sci...333..418P|s2cid=29615751}} and human interference. Because of the Kuroshio’s strong influence, the climate of Nankii is the wettest in the Earth’s {{ill|subtropics|en|subtropics|WD=Q16305538|lt=subtropics}} with rainfall in the southern mountains believed to reach {{convert|5|m|in|-1}} per year and averaging {{convert|3.85|m|in|1}} in the southeastern town of [[:en:Owase, Mie|Owase]], comparable to {{ill|Ketchikan, Alaska|en|Ketchikan, Alaska|WD=Q43981|lt=Ketchikan}}, {{ill|Alaska|en|Alaska|WD=Q797|lt=Alaska}} or {{ill|Tortel|en|Tortel|WD=Q2390232|lt=Tortel}} in southern Chile. When {{ill|Typhoon|en|Typhoon|WD=Q140588|lt=Typhoon}}s hit Japan, the Kii Peninsula is typically the worst affected area and daily rainfalls as high as {{convert|940|mm|in|0}} are known so the Kii Peninsula is often referred to as the Typhoon Ginza (after {{ill|Ginza|en|Ginza|WD=Q746052|lt=Ginza}} in {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo}}). The peninsula was severely affected by the {{ill|Tropical Storm Talas (2011)|en|Tropical Storm Talas (2011)|WD=Q1360941|lt=2011 typhoon season}}, which caused severe damage and many deaths. A significant amount of damage remains today from the 2011 landslides caused by the typhoons.{{Cite web |title=Typhoon in the Kii Peninsula, Japan, September 2011 {{!}} GSI HOME PAGE |url=https://www.gsi.go.jp/kokusaikoryu/kokusaikoryu-e30008.html |access-date=2023-04-26 |website=www.gsi.go.jp}} The natural landscape of the Kii Peninsula is dense {{ill|temperate rainforest|en|temperate rainforest|WD=Q845725|lt=temperate rainforest}}, but the vast majority of forests are monoculture pine plantations which were planted to rebuild after the destruction of World War II. The region is well known for its citrus varieties and orchards. Much of the coast consists of networks of small {{ill|ria|en|ria|WD=Q554394|lt=ria}}s into which flow very steep and rapid streams characterised by numerous high {{ill|Waterfall|en|Waterfall|WD=Q34038|lt=Waterfall}}s. {{ill|Forestry|en|Forestry|WD=Q38112|lt=Forestry}} and {{ill|Fishing|en|Fishing|WD=Q14373|lt=Fishing}} were the traditional economic mainstays of the region and remain important even today despite a declining population and labour force. The region is affected by the dual-crises of severe population decline and widespread poverty.Kozue Taguchi; “Local Forestry and Sawmill Industries; The Case of Kumano, Mie Prefecture”, in Yoshiya Iwai; ''Forestry and the Forest Industry in Japan''; pp. 230–237. {{ISBN|0-7748-0883-7}} ==Location== {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}} occupies much of the area, including the entire southern part. To the northwest of Wakayama Prefecture is {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture}}, whose southern part is on the peninsula. East of Osaka Prefecture is landlocked {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture}}; farther east is {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}}. The [[Seto Inland Sea]] lies to the west of the Kii Peninsula. To the south and east is the Pacific Ocean and to the north is the valley of the {{ill|Kiso Three Rivers|en|Kiso Three Rivers|WD=Q852930|lt=Kiso Three Rivers}} and {{ill|Ise Bay|en|Ise Bay|WD=Q1368308|lt=Ise Bay}}. ==Notable places== Notable places in the Kii Peninsula include: * {{ill|Ise, Mie|en|Ise, Mie|WD=Q328067|lt=Ise}}, the location of the [[:en:Grand Shrine of Ise|Grand Shrine of Ise]] and center of pearl production. * [[Kumano Region]], home of the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Shrine]]s, the World Heritage [[Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodo]] pilgrimage routes and [[Nachi Falls]]. Another name is [[:en:Muro District|Muro District]]. * {{ill|Kushimoto, Wakayama|en|Kushimoto, Wakayama|WD=Q1343733|lt=Kushimoto, Wakayama}}, the southernmost point in Honshū. * {{ill|Mount Kōya|en|Mount Kōya|WD=Q535065|lt=Mount Kōya}} (or Kōyasan), the headquarters of the Shingon sect of Buddhism. * [[:en:Matsuzaka, Mie|Matsuzaka]], now the center of a major beef-producing area, formerly the center of [[Ise merchant]]s. * {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara}}, former [[:en:Capital (political)|capital]] of Japan. * {{ill|Shirahama, Wakayama|en|Shirahama, Wakayama|WD=Q1202728|lt=Shirahama}}, a resort town famous for its beachside onsens. * {{ill|Taiji, Wakayama|en|Taiji, Wakayama|WD=Q1023770|lt=Taiji, Wakayama}}, the birthplace of Japanese traditional whaling. * {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama}}, former home of the Kii (or Kishu) [[Tokugawa clan]]. It is the location of the [[Hinokuma Shrine]], which is affiliated to the Grand Shrine of Ise. * {{ill|Yoshino District, Nara|en|Yoshino District, Nara|WD=Q1154558|lt=Yoshino District}}, a wild area of heavily forested deep mountains, home of the Southern Imperial Court during the [[Nanboku-chō]] period of Japanese history. The Kii Peninsula is the location of a {{ill|UNESCO|en|UNESCO|WD=Q7809|lt=UNESCO}} {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|WD=Q9259|lt=World Heritage Site}}: {{ill|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|en|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|WD=Q611345|lt=Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range}}.[https://whc.unesco.org/en/list/1142 The List] In 2004, [[:en:United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization|UNESCO]] designated three other locations on the Kii Peninsula as World Heritage Sites. They are: #{{ill|Yoshino District, Nara|en|Yoshino District, Nara|WD=Q1154558|lt=Yoshino}} and [[:en:Mount Omine|Mount Omine]], mountainous areas in the north of the peninsula. #[[Kumano shrine|Kumano Shrine]]s, three shrines at the southern tip of the peninsula. #{{ill|Mount Kōya|en|Mount Kōya|WD=Q535065|lt=Mount Kōya}}, the mountain at the west of the peninsula. ==Transportation== * [[:en:Nanki-Shirahama Airport|Nanki-Shirahama Airport]] in {{ill|Shirahama, Wakayama|en|Shirahama, Wakayama|WD=Q1202728|lt=Shirahama}} serves the southern part of the Kii Peninsula. * The {{ill|Kisei Main Line|en|Kisei Main Line|WD=Q582803|lt=Kisei Main Line}} connects Wakayama to Mie Prefecture and runs along the peninsula's coastline. ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==External links== * [https://en.visitwakayama.jp/ Visit Wakayama] * [http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/index.html#2 Tanabe City Kumano Tourism Bureau] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110925191301/http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/index.html#2 |date=2011-09-25 }} [[en:Kii Peninsula]] [[ar:شبه جزيرة كي]] [[az:Kii yarımadası]] [[bg:Кии (полуостров)]] [[ceb:Kii Hantō]] [[cs:Kii (poloostrov)]] [[de:Kii-Halbinsel]] [[es:Península de Kii]] [[eu:Kii penintsula]] [[fa:شبه‌جزیره کی]] [[fi:Kiin niemimaa]] [[fr:Péninsule de Kii]] [[he:חצי האי קיאי]] [[hr:Kii]] [[id:Semenanjung Kii]] [[it:Penisola di Kii]] [[ja:紀伊半島]] [[ko:기이반도]] [[lt:Kijo pusiasalis]] [[nl:Kii (schiereiland)]] [[pl:Kii (półwysep)]] [[pt:Península de Kii]] [[ru:Кии (полуостров)]] [[simple:Kii Peninsula]] [[sv:Kiihalvön]] [[th:คาบสมุทรคิอิ]] [[tr:Kii Yarımadası]] [[vi:Bán đảo Kii]] [[zh:紀伊半島]] {{wikidata link|Q1053290}} [[Category:Kii Peninsula]] [[Category:Landforms of Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Landforms of Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Landforms of Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]] [[Category:Peninsulas of Japan]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages]]

[[ar:شبه جزيرة كي]][[az:Kii yarımadası]][[bg:Кии (полуостров)]][[ceb:Kii Hantō]][[cs:Kii (poloostrov)]][[de:Kii-Halbinsel]][[en:Kii Peninsula]][[es:Península de Kii]][[eu:Kii (schiereiland)]][[pl:Kii (półwysep)]][[pt:Península de Kii]][[ru:Кии (полуостров)]][[simple:Kii Peninsula]][[sv:Kiihalvön]][[th:คาบสมุทรคิอิ]][[tr:Kii Yarımadası]][[vi:Bán đảo Kii]][[zh:紀伊半島]] {{short description|Largest peninsula on the island of Honshū in Japan.}} {{Infobox landform | water = | name = Kii Peninsula | other_name = | type = {{ill|Peninsula|en|Peninsula|WD=Q34763|lt=Peninsula|1=Peninsula}} | photo = Daimonzaka24 2048.jpg | photo_width = | photo_alt = | photo_caption = | map = | relief = | map_image = | map_caption = Location of the Kii Peninsula in Japan | location = [[:en:Kansai Region|Kansai Region]]: {{Plainlist| * {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|1=Wakayama Prefecture}} * {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture|1=Mie Prefecture}} * {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture|1=Osaka Prefecture}} * {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}}}} | coordinates = {{Coord|34|18|10|N|135|57|18|E|region:JP_type:isle|display=it}} | coordinates_ref = | range = | part_of = | water_bodies = | elevation_ft = | elevation_ref = | surface_elevation_ft = | surface_elevation_ref = | highest_point = | highest_elevation = | highest_coords = | length = | width = | area = | depth = | drop = | formed_by = | geology = | age = | orogeny = | volcanic_arc/belt = | volcanic_arc = | volcanic_belt = | volcanic_field = | eruption = | last_eruption = | topo = | operator = | designation = | free_label_1 = Rivers | free_data_1 = {{ill|Kinokawa River|en|Kinokawa River|WD=Q1042624|lt=Kinokawa River|1=Kinokawa River}}, {{ill|Kushida River|en|Kushida River|WD=Q6447936|lt=Kushida River|1=Kushida River}} | free_label_2 = | free_data_2 = | website = | embed = }} The {{Nihongo|'''Kii Peninsula'''|紀伊半島|Kii Hantō|lead=yes}} is the largest {{ill|Peninsula|en|Peninsula|WD=Q34763|lt=Peninsula|1=Peninsula}} on the island of [[Honshu|Honshū]] in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} and is located within the Kansai region.{{Cite web |url=http://www.coremoc.go.jp/english/pub/coralreefjapan/060203_kii.pdf |title=Kii Peninsula |access-date=2011-11-25 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120426001912/http://www.coremoc.go.jp/english/pub/coralreefjapan/060203_kii.pdf |archive-date=2012-04-26 |url-status=dead }} It is named after the ancient {{ill|Kii Province|en|Kii Province|WD=Q864053|lt=Kii Province|1=Kii Province}}. The peninsula has long been a sacred place in Buddhism, Shinto, and Shugendo, and many people would visit from all over Japan as part of the Kumano religious practices. == Overview == {{Location map|Japan|width=220|float=left|caption=Location of Kii Peninsula|label=Kii
Peninsula|position=bottom|mark=Red circle.svg|marksize=30|lat_deg=34|lat_min=08|lat_sec=10|lat_dir=N|lon_deg=135|lon_min=53|lon_sec=18|lon_dir=E}} The area south of the “{{ill|Japan Median Tectonic Line|en|Japan Median Tectonic Line|WD=Q1185053|lt=Central Tectonic Line|1=Japan Median Tectonic Line}}” is called {{Nihongo|'''Nanki'''|南紀}}, and is home to reef-like coral communities which are amongst the northernmost in the world{{Cite book|title=Coral Reefs of Japan|publisher=Ministry of the Environment, Japan|year=2004}} (apart from [[:en:cold-water corals|cold-water corals]]{{Cite web|url=https://www.earthrangers.com/risk/coral-reefs-of-canada-pacific-coast-rocky-reefs/|title=Coral Reefs of Canada: Pacific Coast Rocky Reefs {{!}} Earth Rangers: Where kids go to save animals!|last=Rangers|first=Earth|date=2014-04-11|website=Earth Rangers|language=en-US|access-date=2020-01-17}}) due to the presence of the warm {{ill|Kuroshio Current|en|Kuroshio Current|WD=Q53842|lt=Kuroshio Current|1=Kuroshio Current}}, though these are threatened by global warming{{Cite journal|last1=Pandolfi|first1=John M.|last2=Connolly|first2=Sean R.|last3=Marshall|first3=Dustin J.|last4=Cohen|first4=Anne L.|date=2011-07-22|title=Projecting Coral Reef Futures Under Global Warming and Ocean Acidification|url=https://www.science.org/doi/10.1126/science.1204794|journal=Science|language=en|volume=333|issue=6041|pages=418–422|doi=10.1126/science.1204794|issn=0036-8075|pmid=21778392|bibcode=2011Sci...333..418P|s2cid=29615751}} and human interference. Because of the Kuroshio’s strong influence, the climate of Nankii is the wettest in the Earth’s {{ill|subtropics|en|subtropics|WD=Q16305538|lt=subtropics|1=Subtropics}} with rainfall in the southern mountains believed to reach {{convert|5|m|in|-1}} per year and averaging {{convert|3.85|m|in|1}} in the southeastern town of [[:en:Owase, Mie|Owase]], comparable to {{ill|Ketchikan, Alaska|en|Ketchikan, Alaska|WD=Q43981|lt=Ketchikan|1=Ketchikan, Alaska}}, {{ill|Alaska|en|Alaska|WD=Q797|lt=Alaska|1=Alaska}} or {{ill|Tortel|en|Tortel|WD=Q2390232|lt=Tortel|1=Tortel}} in southern Chile. When {{ill|Typhoon|en|Typhoon|WD=Q140588|lt=Typhoon|1=Typhoon}}s hit Japan, the Kii Peninsula is typically the worst affected area and daily rainfalls as high as {{convert|940|mm|in|0}} are known so the Kii Peninsula is often referred to as the Typhoon Ginza (after {{ill|Ginza|en|Ginza|WD=Q746052|lt=Ginza|1=Ginza}} in {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}}). The peninsula was severely affected by the {{ill|Tropical Storm Talas (2011)|en|Tropical Storm Talas (2011)|WD=Q1360941|lt=2011 typhoon season|1=Tropical Storm Talas (2011)}}, which caused severe damage and many deaths. A significant amount of damage remains today from the 2011 landslides caused by the typhoons.{{Cite web |title=Typhoon in the Kii Peninsula, Japan, September 2011 {{!}} GSI HOME PAGE |url=https://www.gsi.go.jp/kokusaikoryu/kokusaikoryu-e30008.html |access-date=2023-04-26 |website=www.gsi.go.jp}} The natural landscape of the Kii Peninsula is dense {{ill|temperate rainforest|en|temperate rainforest|WD=Q845725|lt=temperate rainforest|1=Temperate rainforest}}, but the vast majority of forests are monoculture pine plantations which were planted to rebuild after the destruction of World War II. The region is well known for its citrus varieties and orchards. Much of the coast consists of networks of small {{ill|ria|en|ria|WD=Q554394|lt=ria|1=Ria}}s into which flow very steep and rapid streams characterised by numerous high {{ill|Waterfall|en|Waterfall|WD=Q34038|lt=Waterfall|1=Waterfall}}s. {{ill|Forestry|en|Forestry|WD=Q38112|lt=Forestry|1=Forestry}} and {{ill|Fishing|en|Fishing|WD=Q14373|lt=Fishing|1=Fishing}} were the traditional economic mainstays of the region and remain important even today despite a declining population and labour force. The region is affected by the dual-crises of severe population decline and widespread poverty.Kozue Taguchi; “Local Forestry and Sawmill Industries; The Case of Kumano, Mie Prefecture”, in Yoshiya Iwai; ''Forestry and the Forest Industry in Japan''; pp. 230–237. {{ISBN|0-7748-0883-7}} ==Location== {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|1=Wakayama Prefecture}} occupies much of the area, including the entire southern part. To the northwest of Wakayama Prefecture is {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture|1=Osaka Prefecture}}, whose southern part is on the peninsula. East of Osaka Prefecture is landlocked {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}}; farther east is {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture|1=Mie Prefecture}}. The [[Seto Inland Sea]] lies to the west of the Kii Peninsula. To the south and east is the Pacific Ocean and to the north is the valley of the {{ill|Kiso Three Rivers|en|Kiso Three Rivers|WD=Q852930|lt=Kiso Three Rivers|1=Kiso Three Rivers}} and {{ill|Ise Bay|en|Ise Bay|WD=Q1368308|lt=Ise Bay|1=Ise Bay}}. ==Notable places== Notable places in the Kii Peninsula include: * {{ill|Ise, Mie|en|Ise, Mie|WD=Q328067|lt=Ise|1=Ise, Mie}}, the location of the [[:en:Grand Shrine of Ise|Grand Shrine of Ise]] and center of pearl production. * [[Kumano Region]], home of the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Shrine]]s, the World Heritage [[Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodo]] pilgrimage routes and [[Nachi Falls]]. Another name is [[:en:Muro District|Muro District]]. * {{ill|Kushimoto, Wakayama|en|Kushimoto, Wakayama|WD=Q1343733|lt=Kushimoto, Wakayama|1=Kushimoto, Wakayama}}, the southernmost point in Honshū. * {{ill|Mount Kōya|en|Mount Kōya|WD=Q535065|lt=Mount Kōya|1=Mount Kōya}} (or Kōyasan), the headquarters of the Shingon sect of Buddhism. * [[:en:Matsuzaka, Mie|Matsuzaka]], now the center of a major beef-producing area, formerly the center of [[Ise merchant]]s. * {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara|1=Nara (city)}}, former [[:en:Capital (political)|capital]] of Japan. * {{ill|Shirahama, Wakayama|en|Shirahama, Wakayama|WD=Q1202728|lt=Shirahama|1=Shirahama, Wakayama}}, a resort town famous for its beachside onsens. * {{ill|Taiji, Wakayama|en|Taiji, Wakayama|WD=Q1023770|lt=Taiji, Wakayama|1=Taiji, Wakayama}}, the birthplace of Japanese traditional whaling. * {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama|1=Wakayama Prefecture}}, former home of the Kii (or Kishu) [[Tokugawa clan]]. It is the location of the [[Hinokuma Shrine]], which is affiliated to the Grand Shrine of Ise. * {{ill|Yoshino District, Nara|en|Yoshino District, Nara|WD=Q1154558|lt=Yoshino District|1=Yoshino District, Nara}}, a wild area of heavily forested deep mountains, home of the Southern Imperial Court during the [[Nanboku-chō]] period of Japanese history. The Kii Peninsula is the location of a {{ill|UNESCO|en|UNESCO|WD=Q7809|lt=UNESCO|1=UNESCO}} {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|WD=Q9259|lt=World Heritage Site|1=World Heritage Site}}: {{ill|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|en|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|WD=Q611345|lt=Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|1=Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range}}.[https://whc.unesco.org/en/list/1142 The List] In 2004, [[:en:United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization|UNESCO]] designated three other locations on the Kii Peninsula as World Heritage Sites. They are: #{{ill|Yoshino District, Nara|en|Yoshino District, Nara|WD=Q1154558|lt=Yoshino|1=Yoshino District, Nara}} and [[:en:Mount Omine|Mount Omine]], mountainous areas in the north of the peninsula. #[[Kumano shrine|Kumano Shrine]]s, three shrines at the southern tip of the peninsula. #{{ill|Mount Kōya|en|Mount Kōya|WD=Q535065|lt=Mount Kōya|1=Mount Kōya}}, the mountain at the west of the peninsula. ==Transportation== * [[:en:Nanki-Shirahama Airport|Nanki-Shirahama Airport]] in {{ill|Shirahama, Wakayama|en|Shirahama, Wakayama|WD=Q1202728|lt=Shirahama|1=Shirahama, Wakayama}} serves the southern part of the Kii Peninsula. * The {{ill|Kisei Main Line|en|Kisei Main Line|WD=Q582803|lt=Kisei Main Line|1=Kisei Main Line}} connects Wakayama to Mie Prefecture and runs along the peninsula's coastline. ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==External links== * [https://en.visitwakayama.jp/ Visit Wakayama] * [http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/index.html#2 Tanabe City Kumano Tourism Bureau] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110925191301/http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/index.html#2 |date=2011-09-25 }} [[en:Kii Peninsula]] [[ar:شبه جزيرة كي]] [[az:Kii yarımadası]] [[bg:Кии | designation = | free_label_1 = Rivers | free_data_1 = {{ill|Kinokawa River|en|Kinokawa River|WD=Q1042624|lt=Kinokawa River|1=Kinokawa River}}, {{ill|Kushida River|en|Kushida River|WD=Q6447936|lt=Kushida River|1=Kushida River}} | free_label_2 = | free_data_2 = | website = | embed = }} The {{Nihongo|'''Kii Peninsula'''|紀伊半島|Kii Hantō|lead=yes}} is the largest {{ill|Peninsula|en|Peninsula|WD=Q34763|lt=Peninsula|1=Peninsula}} on the island of [[Honshu|Honshū]] in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} and is located within the Kansai region.{{Cite web |url=http://www.coremoc.go.jp/english/pub/coralreefjapan/060203_kii.pdf |title=Kii Peninsula |access-date=2011-11-25 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120426001912/http://www.coremoc.go.jp/english/pub/coralreefjapan/060203_kii.pdf |archive-date=2012-04-26 |url-status=dead }} It is named after the ancient {{ill|Kii Province|en|Kii Province|WD=Q864053|lt=Kii Province|1=Kii Province}}. The peninsula has long been a sacred place in Buddhism, Shinto, and Shugendo, and many people would visit from all over Japan as part of the Kumano religious practices. == Overview == {{Location map|Japan|width=220|float=left|caption=Location of Kii Peninsula|label=Kii
Peninsula|position=bottom|mark=Red circle.svg|marksize=30|lat_deg=34|lat_min=08|lat_sec=10|lat_dir=N|lon_deg=135|lon_min=53|lon_sec=18|lon_dir=E}} The area south of the “{{ill|Japan Median Tectonic Line|en|Japan Median Tectonic Line|WD=Q1185053|lt=Central Tectonic Line|1=Japan Median Tectonic Line}}” is called {{Nihongo|'''Nanki'''|南紀}}, and is home to reef-like coral communities which are amongst the northernmost in the world{{Cite book|title=Coral Reefs of Japan|publisher=Ministry of the Environment, Japan|year=2004}} (apart from [[:en:cold-water corals|cold-water corals]]{{Cite web|url=https://www.earthrangers.com/risk/coral-reefs-of-canada-pacific-coast-rocky-reefs/|title=Coral Reefs of Canada: Pacific Coast Rocky Reefs {{!}} Earth Rangers: Where kids go to save animals!|last=Rangers|first=Earth|date=2014-04-11|website=Earth Rangers|language=en-US|access-date=2020-01-17}}) due to the presence of the warm {{ill|Kuroshio Current|en|Kuroshio Current|WD=Q53842|lt=Kuroshio Current|1=Kuroshio Current}}, though these are threatened by global warming{{Cite journal|last1=Pandolfi|first1=John M.|last2=Connolly|first2=Sean R.|last3=Marshall|first3=Dustin J.|last4=Cohen|first4=Anne L.|date=2011-07-22|title=Projecting Coral Reef Futures Under Global Warming and Ocean Acidification|url=https://www.science.org/doi/10.1126/science.1204794|journal=Science|language=en|volume=333|issue=6041|pages=418–422|doi=10.1126/science.1204794|issn=0036-8075|pmid=21778392|bibcode=2011Sci...333..418P|s2cid=29615751}} and human interference. Because of the Kuroshio’s strong influence, the climate of Nankii is the wettest in the Earth’s {{ill|subtropics|en|subtropics|WD=Q16305538|lt=subtropics|1=Subtropics}} with rainfall in the southern mountains believed to reach {{convert|5|m|in|-1}} per year and averaging {{convert|3.85|m|in|1}} in the southeastern town of [[:en:Owase, Mie|Owase]], comparable to {{ill|Ketchikan, Alaska|en|Ketchikan, Alaska|WD=Q43981|lt=Ketchikan|1=Ketchikan, Alaska}}, {{ill|Alaska|en|Alaska|WD=Q797|lt=Alaska|1=Alaska}} or {{ill|Tortel|en|Tortel|WD=Q2390232|lt=Tortel|1=Tortel}} in southern Chile. When {{ill|Typhoon|en|Typhoon|WD=Q140588|lt=Typhoon|1=Typhoon}}s hit Japan, the Kii Peninsula is typically the worst affected area and daily rainfalls as high as {{convert|940|mm|in|0}} are known so the Kii Peninsula is often referred to as the Typhoon Ginza (after {{ill|Ginza|en|Ginza|WD=Q746052|lt=Ginza|1=Ginza}} in {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}}). The peninsula was severely affected by the {{ill|Tropical Storm Talas (2011)|en|Tropical Storm Talas (2011)|WD=Q1360941|lt=2011 typhoon season|1=Tropical Storm Talas (2011)}}, which caused severe damage and many deaths. A significant amount of damage remains today from the 2011 landslides caused by the typhoons.{{Cite web |title=Typhoon in the Kii Peninsula, Japan, September 2011 {{!}} GSI HOME PAGE |url=https://www.gsi.go.jp/kokusaikoryu/kokusaikoryu-e30008.html |access-date=2023-04-26 |website=www.gsi.go.jp}} The natural landscape of the Kii Peninsula is dense {{ill|temperate rainforest|en|temperate rainforest|WD=Q845725|lt=temperate rainforest|1=Temperate rainforest}}, but the vast majority of forests are monoculture pine plantations which were planted to rebuild after the destruction of World War II. The region is well known for its citrus varieties and orchards. Much of the coast consists of networks of small {{ill|ria|en|ria|WD=Q554394|lt=ria|1=Ria}}s into which flow very steep and rapid streams characterised by numerous high {{ill|Waterfall|en|Waterfall|WD=Q34038|lt=Waterfall|1=Waterfall}}s. {{ill|Forestry|en|Forestry|WD=Q38112|lt=Forestry|1=Forestry}} and {{ill|Fishing|en|Fishing|WD=Q14373|lt=Fishing|1=Fishing}} were the traditional economic mainstays of the region and remain important even today despite a declining population and labour force. The region is affected by the dual-crises of severe population decline and widespread poverty.Kozue Taguchi; “Local Forestry and Sawmill Industries; The Case of Kumano, Mie Prefecture”, in Yoshiya Iwai; ''Forestry and the Forest Industry in Japan''; pp. 230–237. {{ISBN|0-7748-0883-7}} ==Location== {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|1=Wakayama Prefecture}} occupies much of the area, including the entire southern part. To the northwest of Wakayama Prefecture is {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture|1=Osaka Prefecture}}, whose southern part is on the peninsula. East of Osaka Prefecture is landlocked {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}}; farther east is {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture|1=Mie Prefecture}}. The [[Seto Inland Sea]] lies to the west of the Kii Peninsula. To the south and east is the Pacific Ocean and to the north is the valley of the {{ill|Kiso Three Rivers|en|Kiso Three Rivers|WD=Q852930|lt=Kiso Three Rivers|1=Kiso Three Rivers}} and {{ill|Ise Bay|en|Ise Bay|WD=Q1368308|lt=Ise Bay|1=Ise Bay}}. ==Notable places== Notable places in the Kii Peninsula include: * {{ill|Ise, Mie|en|Ise, Mie|WD=Q328067|lt=Ise|1=Ise, Mie}}, the location of the [[:en:Grand Shrine of Ise|Grand Shrine of Ise]] and center of pearl production. * [[Kumano Region]], home of the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Shrine]]s, the World Heritage [[Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodo]] pilgrimage routes and [[Nachi Falls]]. Another name is [[:en:Muro District|Muro District]]. * {{ill|Kushimoto, Wakayama|en|Kushimoto, Wakayama|WD=Q1343733|lt=Kushimoto, Wakayama|1=Kushimoto, Wakayama}}, the southernmost point in Honshū. * {{ill|Mount Kōya|en|Mount Kōya|WD=Q535065|lt=Mount Kōya|1=Mount Kōya}} (or Kōyasan), the headquarters of the Shingon sect of Buddhism. * [[:en:Matsuzaka, Mie|Matsuzaka]], now the center of a major beef-producing area, formerly the center of [[Ise merchant]]s. * {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara|1=Nara (city)}}, former [[:en:Capital (political)|capital]] of Japan. * {{ill|Shirahama, Wakayama|en|Shirahama, Wakayama|WD=Q1202728|lt=Shirahama|1=Shirahama, Wakayama}}, a resort town famous for its beachside onsens. * {{ill|Taiji, Wakayama|en|Taiji, Wakayama|WD=Q1023770|lt=Taiji, Wakayama|1=Taiji, Wakayama}}, the birthplace of Japanese traditional whaling. * {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama|1=Wakayama Prefecture}}, former home of the Kii (or Kishu) [[Tokugawa clan]]. It is the location of the [[Hinokuma Shrine]], which is affiliated to the Grand Shrine of Ise. * {{ill|Yoshino District, Nara|en|Yoshino District, Nara|WD=Q1154558|lt=Yoshino District|1=Yoshino District, Nara}}, a wild area of heavily forested deep mountains, home of the Southern Imperial Court during the [[Nanboku-chō]] period of Japanese history. The Kii Peninsula is the location of a {{ill|UNESCO|en|UNESCO|WD=Q7809|lt=UNESCO|1=UNESCO}} {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|WD=Q9259|lt=World Heritage Site|1=World Heritage Site}}: {{ill|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|en|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|WD=Q611345|lt=Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|1=Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range}}.[https://whc.unesco.org/en/list/1142 The List] In 2004, [[:en:United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization|UNESCO]] designated three other locations on the Kii Peninsula as World Heritage Sites. They are: #{{ill|Yoshino District, Nara|en|Yoshino District, Nara|WD=Q1154558|lt=Yoshino|1=Yoshino District, Nara}} and [[:en:Mount Omine|Mount Omine]], mountainous areas in the north of the peninsula. #[[Kumano shrine|Kumano Shrine]]s, three shrines at the southern tip of the peninsula. #{{ill|Mount Kōya|en|Mount Kōya|WD=Q535065|lt=Mount Kōya|1=Mount Kōya}}, the mountain at the west of the peninsula. ==Transportation== * [[:en:Nanki-Shirahama Airport|Nanki-Shirahama Airport]] in {{ill|Shirahama, Wakayama|en|Shirahama, Wakayama|WD=Q1202728|lt=Shirahama|1=Shirahama, Wakayama}} serves the southern part of the Kii Peninsula. * The {{ill|Kisei Main Line|en|Kisei Main Line|WD=Q582803|lt=Kisei Main Line|1=Kisei Main Line}} connects Wakayama to Mie Prefecture and runs along the peninsula's coastline. ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==External links== * [https://en.visitwakayama.jp/ Visit Wakayama] * [http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/index.html#2 Tanabe City Kumano Tourism Bureau] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110925191301/http://www.tb-kumano.jp/en/index.html#2 |date=2011-09-25 }} [[en:Kii Peninsula]] [[ar:شبه جزيرة كي]] [[az:Kii yarımadası]] [[bg:Кии (полуостров)]] [[ceb:Kii Hantō]] [[cs:Kii (poloostrov)]] [[de:Kii-Halbinsel]] [[es:Península de Kii]] [[eu:Kii penintsula]] [[fa:شبه‌جزیره کی]] [[fi:Kiin niemimaa]] [[fr:Péninsule de Kii]] [[he:חצי
